attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

@@ -10,7 +10,8 @@ It is a single self-contained Go binary that runs on both ends of the tunnel:
 
 - **edge** — a Linux box (behind a router) that bonds the local WAN uplinks;
 - **concentrator** — a small public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel and NATs

 The same binary serves both roles; the role is chosen from the config file.
 
@@ -37,9 +38,11 @@ WireGuard engine (TUN, Noise handshake, AEAD, rekey, roaming, keepalive) and put
 **all** bonding logic — multipath scheduling, an obfuscated outer frame codec,
 Reed-Solomon FEC, a receive resequencer, and per-path telemetry — into a custom
 `conn.Bind` that lives *beneath* the engine and operates only on opaque, already-

 **[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)**.
 
 ## Quick start
diff --git a/docs/design.md b/docs/design.md
index 3526f97..a6a0d7a 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -119,6 +119,20 @@ The heart of wanbond: the `conn.Bind` implementation the engine drives. It:
 - **learns edge endpoints dynamically** — the concentrator needs no edge endpoint
   config; it discovers each path's (possibly NAT'd) source from inbound traffic,
   enabling real CGNAT traversal.
+- **demuxes multiple peers by authenticated source binding (G4 multi-peer)** — on a
+  concentrator with multiple edges, inbound datagrams are routed to the owning peer
+  via `peerBySource`, an atomic-pointer source-address map populated only from
+  authenticated PROBE frames. Each peer holds its own PSK; the first PROBE from a
+  source that MAC-verifies under a peer's PSK binds that source to that peer —
+  subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from the same source are routed without
+  re-authentication, keeping the receive hot path fast. Per-peer PSK is optional in
+  single-peer configs (uses the top-level PSK for back-compat) and required in
+  multi-peer mode (each edge gets a distinct PSK so the concentrator can
+  discriminate them by PROBE authentication alone, with no reliance on tunnel
+  addresses that cross inside the obfuscated outer frame). Binding is learned only
+  from PROBE frames — unauthenticated DATA/PARITY cannot establish or hijack a
+  source-to-peer binding (D9/D11). Per-peer name (optional in single-peer, required
+  in multi-peer) is exposed in metrics labels and logs for operational clarity.
 - owns the **per-path UDP sockets**, byte counters, the send-path FEC `Admit`, the
   adaptive-FEC tick loop, and the wiring that hands frames to the scheduler and
   the resequencer.
@@ -450,12 +464,28 @@ misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.
   WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
 - **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
   monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
+  - **Per-peer PSK (multi-peer concentrator, G4):** each edge gets a distinct PSK
+    to decrypt its own PROBE frames. The concentrator uses PROBE MAC-verification
+    to learn each source address's owning peer (`peerBySource` binding in
+    `internal/bind/multipath.go`); a source that MAC-verifies under peer A's PSK
+    is bound to A, and subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from it route to A without
+    re-authentication. An attacker cannot forge a PROBE that verifies under a peer
+    they do not have the PSK for, so source-to-peer bindings are authenticated by
+    design. Single-peer configs remain back-compatible: the per-peer PSK field is
+    optional (uses the top-level PSK as default), so existing single-peer
+    deployments parse and run identically unchanged.
 - **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
   attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
   payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
   risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
   a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
   the hot path.
+  - **In multi-peer mode**, unauthenticated DATA/PARITY do not establish
+    source-to-peer bindings (they route using existing bindings learned from
+    PROBE frames). An attacker can spam DATA frames from a forged source address
+    and waste resequence/FEC work if that address has no binding, but cannot
+    impersonate an existing peer (its source bindings are locked by authenticated
+    PROBEs).
 - **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
   obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
   Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index 5300168..a2a0460 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -129,6 +129,58 @@ ends). Omit it entirely for plain WireGuard framing.
 Generate keys with standard WireGuard tooling (`wg genkey | tee k | wg pubkey`)
 and the PSK with `head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64`.
 
+### Multi-peer concentrator (G4) — supporting multiple edges
+
+A concentrator can bond traffic from **multiple edges** (e.g., a branch-office
+edge, a mobile edge, a cloud gateway). Plural `[[wireguard.peers]]` blocks are
+supported; each edge gets its own PSK for authenticated source-based demux:
+
+```toml
+role = "concentrator"
+psk = "<base64 default PSK, used by single-peer edges for back-compat>"
+
+[[paths]]
+name = "wan0"
+source_addr = "10.0.0.5"
+
+[wireguard]
+private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
+listen_port = 51820
+
+# Edge 1: office with its own PSK
+[[wireguard.peers]]
+public_key = "<base64 office-edge public key>"
+allowed_ips = ["10.77.1.0/24"]
+psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for office-edge>"   # REQUIRED in multi-peer
+name = "office"                                 # REQUIRED in multi-peer
+
+# Edge 2: mobile with a different PSK
+[[wireguard.peers]]
+public_key = "<base64 mobile-edge public key>"
+allowed_ips = ["10.77.2.0/24"]
+psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for mobile-edge>"   # Must differ from office PSK
+name = "mobile"                                 # Must differ from office name
+
+[metrics]
+listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
+```
+
+**Single-peer back-compatibility:** when a concentrator has only one peer
+(`[[wireguard.peers]]`), the per-peer PSK field is optional — it defaults to
+the top-level `psk`, so existing single-peer configs parse and run unchanged.
+
+**Multi-peer requirements:** with more than one peer, each peer **must** have
+both a unique `psk` (for authenticated PROBE demux) and a unique `name` (for
+metrics/logging clarity). The concentrator uses the per-peer PSK to verify
+PROBE frames and bind each source address to its owning peer; subsequent
+DATA/PARITY frames from that source route accordingly without re-authentication,
+keeping the receive fast path efficient.
+
+**Edges see no change:** each edge always points to the concentrator's single
+virtual endpoint (its public IP + port); it does not know or care that multiple
+edges are connected to the same concentrator. The concentrator's internal demux
+is entirely transparent to the edge.
+
 ### Optional `[fec]` forward-error-correction plane
 
 FEC is **off** unless an `[fec]` block is present. A fixed-ratio block protects
diff --git a/wanbond.example.toml b/wanbond.example.toml
index 5886214..89732fc 100644
--- a/wanbond.example.toml
+++ b/wanbond.example.toml
@@ -102,6 +102,60 @@ allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
                                    #   Rejected on the concentrator. Config-surface
                                    #   only today: it does not itself install any
                                    #   OS-level default route.
+# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # OPTIONAL, CONCENTRATOR-ONLY (multi-peer). Per-peer
+                                   #   override of the top-level outer-control PSK.
+                                   #   With a single configured peer, leave it UNSET:
+                                   #   the top-level Config.PSK remains the default,
+                                   #   so a legacy single-peer config carries only the
+                                   #   top-level `psk` and behaves byte-identically to
+                                   #   before this field existed. With more than one
+                                   #   peer, this field is REQUIRED and must be present
+                                   #   and pairwise-distinct across peers — the
+                                   #   top-level psk alone cannot discriminate which
+                                   #   peer authenticated an inbound frame (T81, Q21).
+                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
+# name = "edge1"                    # OPTIONAL, CONCENTRATOR-ONLY (multi-peer). Human-
+                                   #   readable identifier, analogous to Path.Name.
+                                   #   With a single configured peer, it is unused and
+                                   #   optional. With more than one peer, this field
+                                   #   is REQUIRED and must be unique across peers
+                                   #   (T81, Q21). Exposed in metrics labels and logs
+                                   #   for clarity when there are multiple edges.
+
+# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
+# MULTI-PEER CONCENTRATOR EXAMPLE (G4): TWO EDGES, ONE CONCENTRATOR
+# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
+#
+# The plural [[wireguard.peers]] syntax (one block per edge) is supported on the
+# concentrator. Each edge gets its own PSK for authenticated demux (source-based
+# binding at PROBE frame ingress) and a unique name for metrics/logging.
+# Single-peer configs (one [[wireguard.peers]] block) work unchanged.
+#
+# # Concentrator: two-edge configuration
+# role = "concentrator"
+# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK, default for single-peer edges (back-compat)>"
+#
+# [[paths]]
+# name = "wan0"
+# source_addr = "10.0.0.5"
+#
+# [wireguard]
+# private_key = "…"
+# listen_port = 51820
+#
+# # Edge 1: small office, its own PSK
+# [[wireguard.peers]]
+# public_key = "<base64 office edge public key>"
+# allowed_ips = ["10.77.1.0/24"]
+# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for edge1>"          # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
+# name = "office-edge"                             # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
+#
+# # Edge 2: mobile network, its own PSK
+# [[wireguard.peers]]
+# public_key = "<base64 mobile edge public key>"
+# allowed_ips = ["10.77.2.0/24"]
+# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for edge2>"          # Must differ from edge1's PSK

+### Multi-peer concentrator (G4) — supporting multiple edges
+
+A concentrator can bond traffic from **multiple edges** (e.g., a branch-office
+edge, a mobile edge, a cloud gateway). Plural `[[wireguard.peers]]` blocks are
+supported; each edge gets its own PSK for authenticated source-based demux:
+
+```toml
+role = "concentrator"
+psk = "<base64 default PSK, used by single-peer edges for back-compat>"
+
+[[paths]]
+name = "wan0"
+source_addr = "10.0.0.5"
+
+[wireguard]
+private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
+listen_port = 51820
+
+# Edge 1: office with its own PSK
+[[wireguard.peers]]
+public_key = "<base64 office-edge public key>"
+allowed_ips = ["10.77.1.0/24"]
+psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for office-edge>"   # REQUIRED in multi-peer
+name = "office"                                 # REQUIRED in multi-peer
+
+# Edge 2: mobile with a different PSK
+[[wireguard.peers]]
+public_key = "<base64 mobile-edge public key>"
+allowed_ips = ["10.77.2.0/24"]
+psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for mobile-edge>"   # Must differ from office PSK
+name = "mobile"                                 # Must differ from office name
+
+[metrics]
+listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
+```
+
+**Single-peer back-compatibility:** when a concentrator has only one peer
+(`[[wireguard.peers]]`), the per-peer PSK field is optional — it defaults to
+the top-level `psk`, so existing single-peer configs parse and run unchanged.
+
+**Multi-peer requirements:** with more than one peer, each peer **must** have
+both a unique `psk` (for authenticated PROBE demux) and a unique `name` (for
+metrics/logging clarity). The concentrator uses the per-peer PSK to verify
+PROBE frames and bind each source address to its owning peer; subsequent
+DATA/PARITY frames from that source route accordingly without re-authentication,
+keeping the receive fast path efficient.
+
+**Edges see no change:** each edge always points to the concentrator's single
+virtual endpoint (its public IP + port); it does not know or care that multiple
+edges are connected to the same concentrator. The concentrator's internal demux
+is entirely transparent to the edge.
+
 ### Optional `[fec]` forward-error-correction plane
 
 FEC is **off** unless an `[fec]` block is present. A fixed-ratio block protects

content.text:
All evidence gathered. Cleaning up my transient overlay files is unnecessary (scratchpad-only, no repo edits made).

**Findings, grounded:**

1. **Acceptance clause "PARSES via the config test suite" — unmet.** The multi-peer stanza added to `wanbond.example.toml` is entirely commented out with `<base64 …>` placeholders, and no test anywhere loads `wanbond.example.toml` or a fixture derived from it (`grep -rn "wanbond.example" --include="*.go"` finds only two prose mentions in comments; no `TestExampleConfigLoads` exists). The stanza's *shape* is valid — I verified operationally via a transient overlay test mirroring the install.md stanza with real keys: `config.Load` succeeds — but nothing in the suite covers it, so a future config change could silently invalidate the documented example.

2. **Single-peer per-peer-PSK semantics misdocumented.** `internal/config/config.go` L1132-1135 **rejects** a per-peer `psk` when exactly one peer is configured ("psk is not meaningful with a single peer"). But docs/install.md says "the per-peer PSK field is optional — it defaults to the top-level `psk`", and docs/design.md says (twice) "optional in single-peer configs (uses the top-level PSK for back-compat)". "Optional with a default" and "load error if present" are different contracts. `wanbond.example.toml` calls it a "per-peer override of the top-level outer-control PSK" — override semantics that do not exist (`TestPeerIdentitiesSinglePeerTopLevelPSKWinsOverDistinctPeerPSK` pins that the top-level PSK wins even if set).

3. **Top-level `psk` role on a multi-peer concentrator misdocumented.** Both new stanzas annotate it as "default PSK, used by single-peer edges for back-compat". Reality: `device.go` L283-317 feeds only `ids[i].PSK` (per-peer PSKs) to the bind; `TestPeerIdentitiesMultiPeerUsesOwnPSKAndName` pins that the top-level psk "must NOT leak into a multi-peer identity". On a multi-peer concentrator the top-level psk is required by validate (L1159) but authenticates nothing — an operator could believe an existing edge keeps its old shared PSK after a second peer is added; it cannot.

4. **Peer-name-in-metrics claim overstated.** `bind.Multipath.BoundPeerNames` (multipath.go L2608-2622): the primary peer (peers[0]) is **always** named `""`, "on both the single-peer edge/hub AND a multi-peer concentrator" — only peers registered via `AddConcentratorPeer` carry their configured names (fixture `{"", "edge2"}` in `TestExpositionTwoPeerSeries`). Docs claim each peer's required unique name is "exposed in metrics labels". The first configured peer's series carry `peer=""` despite config requiring its name. The docs misstate reality (criticism); the underlying name-drop itself is a pre-existing out-of-scope fault (defect).

What checked out: `peerBySource` PROBE-only binding, no DATA/PARITY binding authority, unauthenticated hot path, multi-peer-is-concentrator-only, uniqueness/distinctness validation messages, README/AGENTS.md wording, no stale "single peer only" claims elsewhere. Check suite (`go build && go vet && go test ./...`) green, exit 0.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T98 round 1 (diff 9c793be..24f871d, worktree agent-a436e61b729c26ad8)
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 5 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** check suite green; documented multi-peer stanza shape loads (verified via overlay test); but no config-suite test covers the example, and four doc claims contradict shipped behavior (single-peer per-peer-psk is rejected not defaulted; top-level psk unused in multi-peer; primary peer's name absent from metrics labels)
- **Issues:** none

  "criticism": [
    "Acceptance clause 'wanbond.example.toml multi-peer example PARSES via the config test suite' is unmet: the stanza is comment-only with placeholder keys and no test loads wanbond.example.toml or a multi-peer fixture derived from it (grep --include='*.go' 'wanbond.example' finds only prose mentions). Add a config-suite test (e.g. TestExampleConfigMultiPeerStanzaLoads) that extracts the commented stanza (strip '# ' prefix), substitutes placeholders with valid keys, and asserts config.Load succeeds — I verified the stanza shape itself loads, so only the test is missing.",
    "Single-peer per-peer-psk semantics misdocumented: internal/config/config.go L1132-1135 REJECTS a per-peer psk with exactly one peer ('psk is not meaningful with a single peer'), but docs/install.md says 'the per-peer PSK field is optional — it defaults to the top-level psk' and docs/design.md says twice 'optional in single-peer configs (uses the top-level PSK for back-compat)'. Rewrite to state it must be ABSENT with a single peer (config load error if set); wanbond.example.toml's 'per-peer override of the top-level outer-control PSK' framing is also wrong — no override semantics exist.",
    "Top-level psk role on a multi-peer concentrator misdocumented: both new stanzas (docs/install.md and wanbond.example.toml) annotate it as 'default PSK, used by single-peer edges for back-compat', but on a multi-peer concentrator only per-peer PSKs feed the bind (internal/device/device.go L283-317; TestPeerIdentitiesMultiPeerUsesOwnPSKAndName pins the top-level psk must NOT leak into any identity).   "summary": "Disapprove round 1: missing config-suite coverage of the multi-peer example stanza plus three factual doc/code mismatches (per-peer-psk single-peer rejection, unused top-level psk in multi-peer, primary peer unlabeled in metrics); one out-of-scope defect filed."
